header.text: General Services Administration (GSA) RFQ1809121 TTS NEXT BPAs Support Area 5 Technical Capability

content.text: AIS reduces delivery risk by treating AI implementation as an accountable product lifecycle, not a one-time model build.
AIS delivers AI governance through an Intake, Build, Monitor, and Audit lifecycle that establishes controls before any model enters production and sustains oversight through retirement.

content.title: Why This Matters
body: The same lifecycle artifacts support executive governance, delivery execution, and evaluator traceability.
bullets: Risk classification is completed before solution design.
Evaluation evidence is captured as delivery proceeds.
Monitoring and audit controls are designed into the operating model.

content.title: Figure 1. AI Delivery Control Model
content.alt_text: Diagram showing Intake, Build, Monitor, and Audit stages with governance checkpoints.
content.replacement_guidance: Insert lifecycle diagram showing stage gates, owner checkpoints, and evidence artifacts.
content.caption: Use this figure to orient evaluators before the detailed governance narrative.

content.title: Table 1. Lifecycle Controls Mapped to Proposal Evidence
rows: Classify risk, assign owner, document data provenance.
Governance intake checklist and approval record.
Create source-cited responses, test prompts, and evaluate outputs.
Traceability matrix, test scripts, and evaluation summary.
Track drift, quality, usage, and incident signals.

cells: Independent readiness review and retained evidence package.
Audit-ready control evidence and remediation log.

content.text: Instruction: Describe the offeror's approach to implementing Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning capabilities within a Federal environment.

content.label: Q1
content.question: How does AIS evaluate when AI is the appropriate solution versus when traditional approaches are more effective?

response.text: AIS uses a decision framework that evaluates task complexity, data maturity, explainability requirements, compliance constraints, and expected operational value before recommending an AI approach.
Use AI when probabilistic reasoning or semantic retrieval improves outcomes.
Use deterministic workflows when rules, auditability, or cost make traditional automation stronger.
